critic_raw: The design is competent but I want to probe the inter-message timing encoding. The transformer uses 'inter-message timestamps encoded as relative seconds since ticket creation.' This is a continuous temporal feature fed to a BERT model. BERT's positional encodings are designed for token positions, not temporal gaps between messages. Is there a risk that the temporal encoding is not properly integrated into the BERT architecture and adds noise rather than signal? Additionally, the baseline features include 'time since last customer reply (binned into categorical features)' — this is similar temporal information but discretized. If the transformer's timing encoding is noisy, the baseline may have a more robust representation of timing information.
defender_raw: The temporal encoding for inter-message timestamps is a design parameter of the BERT fine-tuning — 'temporal encodings added to timestamps' means the implementation adds a learned embedding for the inter-message gap, similar to learned positional encodings in standard BERT. This is a reasonable approach. If the temporal encoding adds noise, the model can learn to down-weight it (attention can ignore features); the baseline's binned categorical timing feature is an equally valid representation. If both representations of timing are noisy, this would hurt both models or neither. The key comparison is whether the full sequential context (all messages, their order, and timing) provides more information than the BoW baseline's metadata features — the temporal encoding is one component of this broader test. Any architectural limitation in temporal encoding would reduce (not inflate) the transformer's advantage, making the comparison conservative.
adjudication_raw: One main concern: temporal encoding integration into BERT architecture may be noisy compared to baseline's discretized timing. Defender correctly notes: (1) learned temporal embeddings are a valid approach, (2) BERT's attention can down-weight noisy features, (3) any limitation reduces transformer advantage (conservative comparison). No methodological flaw. Verdict: defense_wins.
